name: Henbane Witches
description: Henbane (Hyoscyamus niger) holds a prominent, often sinister, position in European folklore and witchcraft traditions. Revered and feared for its potent psychoactive properties, henbane was a key ingredient in witches' flying ointments and potions. Its use dates back to ancient times, with evidence suggesting its presence in rituals and medicinal practices. Henbane contains tropane alkaloids like hyoscyamine, scopolamine, and atropine, which can induce hallucinations, delirium, and a sense of detachment from the body, contributing to the sensation of flight reported by witches. In folklore, henbane is linked to the dark arts, necromancy, and communication with spirits. It was believed to open gateways to other realms and facilitate astral projection. The plant's association with witches stems from its ability to induce altered states of consciousness, which were interpreted as spiritual journeys or encounters with supernatural entities. Henbane was also used in love spells and divinatory practices, although its toxicity made it a dangerous and unpredictable tool. Its use required extensive knowledge and caution, further solidifying its connection with skilled practitioners of magic. The plant's dark reputation is reinforced by its physical characteristics; its foul odor and sticky leaves contribute to its aura of danger and mystique. Henbane's cultural importance lies in its representation of the blurred lines between medicine, magic, and the supernatural in European history.
subtitle: Herb of witches and altered states.

keyMyths.title: Witches' Flying Ointment
keyMyths.description: Henbane was a key ingredient in ointments that witches applied to their skin, allowing them to experience the sensation of flight and astral travel. The alkaloids in henbane induced hallucinations and a detached state, perceived as spiritual journeys.
keyMyths.source: Various historical accounts of witchcraft trials and folklore

sources.author: Nicholas Culpeper
sources.description: A 17th-century herbal providing information on the medicinal and astrological properties of herbs, including henbane.
sources.text: A 17th-century herbal providing information on the medicinal and astrological properties of herbs, including henbane.

sources.author: Carlo Ginzburg
sources.description: Examines the historical and anthropological evidence for the use of psychoactive substances in witchcraft rituals.
sources.text: Examines the historical and anthropological evidence for the use of psychoactive substances in witchcraft rituals.

extendedContent.title: Henbane and Flying Ointments
extendedContent.content: Henbane's association with witchcraft is perhaps most pronounced in its role as a key ingredient in "flying ointments." These topical preparations, concocted by witches, were believed to enable astral projection or the illusion of flight. The tropane alkaloids present in henbane, such as scopolamine and hyoscyamine, induce hallucinogenic and deliriant effects when absorbed through the skin. These effects, combined with other herbs like belladonna and mandrake, would create vivid, dream-like states that were interpreted as spiritual journeys or flights to the Sabbat. The application of these ointments was a carefully guarded secret, with recipes varying from region to region, each designed to induce the desired altered state of consciousness.

extendedContent.title: Henbane in Ritual and Divination
extendedContent.content: Beyond its use in flying ointments, henbane held significance in various ritualistic and divinatory practices. It was sometimes burned as incense to induce trance states or to facilitate communication with spirits. The plant's psychoactive properties were seen as a gateway to the otherworld, allowing practitioners to gain insights into the future or to commune with deities. In some traditions, henbane was associated with specific deities, further solidifying its sacred status. The plant's seeds were also used in divination rituals, with their patterns and movements interpreted to reveal hidden knowledge or predict future events. The inherent risks associated with henbane's toxicity added an element of danger and respect to its use in these practices.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism and Folklore
extendedContent.content: Henbane's symbolism is complex and multifaceted, reflecting both its potent properties and its association with witchcraft. The plant was often linked to death and the underworld, due to its poisonous nature and its ability to induce altered states of consciousness. Its dark, drooping flowers and unpleasant odor further contributed to its sinister image. In some folklore traditions, henbane was believed to be a favorite plant of witches, growing in their gardens and used in their spells. The plant's connection to the night and the moon further reinforced its association with the darker aspects of magic. Despite its negative connotations, henbane was also sometimes seen as a protective herb, capable of warding off evil spirits and curses, highlighting the ambivalent nature of its symbolism.

habitat: Henbane is commonly found in waste places, along roadsides, and in fields throughout Europe and parts of Asia, often preferring disturbed soils.
shortDescription: Henbane, a psychoactive herb, was integral to European witchcraft. It's known for its use in flying ointments and potions, deeply rooted in folklore.
